title: Texas races heat up in high-stakes primary
author: CBS News
fullText: And voters in three states, they head to the polls today for major primary elections, including the highstake Senate race happening in the state of Texas. Democrats are hoping to win a statewide office for the first time since 1994. And it all comes down to which candidate makes it out of today's primaries. Let's bring in CBS News national reporter Karen Wah for us today in Houston. Karen, good to see you. So, razor thin margins, especially in the Democratic race. You attended one of the leading candidates events yesterday. Uh, what was the latest plea to voters? >> Hey, Mugo. So, we attended James Tarico's final two events yesterday. They were both here in Houston. And the Democrat reiterated a lot of the classic lines he's been saying over and over throughout his campaign. Notably, he says he believes this is a race between top versus down rather than left versus right. And as a seminarian, he also frequently quotes this popular Bible verse, "Love thy neighbor." Getting loud cheers every time he denounces ICE. Now, as a Democrat, he believes the key to getting things done is bridging the gap between two parties, finding that middle ground in fighting billionaires who he says are the root of the problem. Whereas his Democratic opponent, Jasmine Crockett, she has a more fiery approach, known for her intense language and sparring with Republicans. We've talked to a lot of voters over the past few days and a lot of them are still undecided between Taler Rico and Crockett. We even talked to this one woman here at this busy Houston polling site this morning and literally standing in line. She still had not made up her mind between the two even though she had a matter of minutes to decide. Listen, >> I am very concerned about the direction that the nation is going in right now. So voting is a critical thing and if anybody is watching this having vote, get up and go vote because you need to vote. If you don't vote, don't complain. And so that race is critical on both sides. Even in the Republican party, you have a battle over there. So you have no excuse. Whether you're a Democrat or Republican or an independent, you need to get out here and vote today. >> Speaking of the other side, we're going to be talking to other Republican voters throughout the day as well to see how they're deciding between the three candidates. ago. >> And Karen, turning over to the Republican race now because there is a chance of a runoff in May. What would that election and the campaigning up to that look like? So, Mugo, in Texas, there's a law that you have to get at least 51% of the vote in an election. And there's a very good likelihood that with three candidates on the Republican side that no one does get 51% of that vote. And for now, Donald Trump has not endorsed has declined to endorse any of the three candidates. We have the incumbent John Cordon who has held this current Senate seat for four terms. That's about 24 years. And he has spent this record amount of money to fight the current attorney general, Ken Paxton, who despite having a number of scandals over the past few years, is relatively well-liked by the MAGA base. And certainly that could be another more intense battle between the two if this goes off into a runoff in May. The third candidate we have is Wesley Hunt. He's a congressman who represents Houston here from Houston. He's the one who could force Paxton and Gordon into that runoff if none of the three get 51% today. Mugo. >> Okay. Karen W in Houston for us today. So many campaign signs behind you. We'll see what happens tonight. Thank you so much. And make sure you come back tonight for our CBS News primary special. Our team will cover the results of today's elections and provide analysis as the numbers come in. And you can stream that starting at 9:00 p.m.
